- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- userinterface-wiki: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- code-edit: Use Fast Apply (edit_file) for precise edits without reading full files.
- mastra: Comprehensive Mastra framework guide. Teaches how to find current documentation, verify API signatures, and build agents and workflows. Covers documentation lookup strategies (embedded docs, remote docs), core concepts (agents vs workflows, tools, memory, RAG), TypeScript requirements, and common patterns. Use this skill for all Mastra development to ensure you're using current APIs from the installed version or latest documentation.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- teach-impeccable: One-time setup that gathers design context for your project and saves it to your AI config file. Run once to establish persistent design guidelines.
- onboard: Designs and improves onboarding flows, empty states, and first-run experiences to help users reach value quickly. Use when the user mentions onboarding, first-time users, empty states, activation, getting started, or new user flows.
- explore: Use this skill when the user asks you something that requires searching a code base
- normalize: Audits and realigns UI to match design system standards, spacing, tokens, and patterns. Use when the user mentions consistency, design drift, mismatched styles, tokens, or wants to bring a feature back in line with the system.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- harden: Improve interface resilience through better error handling, i18n support, text overflow handling, and edge case management. Makes interfaces robust and production-ready. Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, or fix overflow and i18n issues.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- feature-research: Research existing architecture before implementing a complex feature.
- extract: Extract and consolidate reusable components, design tokens, and patterns into your design system. Identifies opportunities for systematic reuse and enriches your component library. Use when the user asks to create components, refactor repeated UI patterns, build a design system, or extract tokens.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context.
- arrange: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- tanstack-start-best-practices: TanStack Start best practices for full-stack React applications. Server functions, middleware, SSR, authentication, and deployment patterns. Activate when building full-stack apps with TanStack Start.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- presentation-design: Design and evaluate presentations that communicate effectively. Use when designing a presentation, creating slides, getting presentation feedback, structuring a talk, or reviewing slides. Keywords: presentation, slides, talk, PowerPoint, Keynote, reveal.js.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

2 3 # Greenroom 4 5 **Software for independent music venues.** 6 7 This is the starter codebase for the Greenroom Applied AI PM case study. 8 9
10 11 --- 12 13 You're looking at a working but mediocre product. It's enough to feel real, but every workflow has gaps. **Your job isn't to fix everything — it's to pick a slice and design it well.** See your case study brief for full instructions. 14 15 ## Before you start 16 17 You'll need: 18 19 1. **Node.js, version 20 or higher** — get it from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org/) (pick the LTS version). Verify with `node -v`. 20 2. **Git** — most computers have it. Verify with `git --version`. If not, install from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com/). 21 3. **A code editor.** [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/) is great. [Cursor](https://cursor.com/) is what we'd reach for if we were doing this case study. 22 4. **A GitHub account.** Free at [github.com](https://github.com/). 23 24 If you're on Windows, run all the commands below in **Git Bash**, **PowerShell**, or **WSL** — not the legacy Command Prompt. 25 26 ## Setup, step by step 27 28 ### 1. Fork this repo to your own GitHub account 29 30 Click the **Fork** button at the top right of [https://github.com/samay-cbh/greenroom-starter](https://github.com/samay-cbh/greenroom-starter). You'll get a copy under your own username. 31 32 > _Why fork?_ A fork is your own copy of the repo. You'll commit your changes there, and submit your fork's URL when you're done. We can see your commit history that way. 33 34 ### 2. Clone your fork to your computer 35 36 ```bash 37 git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/greenroom-starter 38 cd greenroom-starter 39 ``` 40 41 (Replace `YOUR-USERNAME` with your actual GitHub username.) 42 43 ### 3. Install dependencies 44 45 ```bash 46 npm install 47 ``` 48 49 This pulls down all the JavaScript packages the project needs. Takes about 60 seconds. You may see a few warnings — those are normal and safe to ignore. 50 51 ### 4. Start the app 52 53 ```bash 54 npm run dev 55 ``` 56 57 You'll see something like: 58 59 ``` 60 ▲ Next.js 16.x 61 - Local: http://localhost:3000 62 63 ✓ Ready in 1.2s 64 ``` 65 66 ### 5. Open it in your browser 67 68 Go to **[http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000)**. 69 70 You'll land on Mariana's home view at The Crescent. **Click "Where to start" in the sidebar** for an in-product orientation. 71 72 > **Tip:** Press **⌘K** (Mac) or **Ctrl+K** (Windows/Linux) anywhere in the app to open the command palette — search across shows and artists instantly. 73 74 --- 75 76 ## What's running 77 78 You're logged in automatically as **Mariana Reyes**, lead booker at The Crescent (650-cap, Nashville). The product has these surfaces: 79 80 | Route | What it is | 81 |---|---| 82 | `/shows` | Mariana's home view. 24 months of completed shows, searchable and grouped by month. | 83 | `/shows/[id]` | Show detail. Deal terms, artist info, ticket sales, expenses, comps. | 84 | `/shows/[id]/settle` | The in-app settlement worksheet. **Try it on a few shows.** | 85 | `/artists` | Roster of artists who've played the venue, bucketed by frequency. | 86 | `/reports` | Aggregate metrics. The numbers Pri (the CEO) is watching. | 87 | `/context` | Orientation for you, the candidate. Linked from the sidebar. | 88 89 ### Recommended path your first time through 90 91 1. Open `/context` (the sidebar's "Where to start" link). 5-minute tour. 92 2. Then `/shows`. Pick a Vs-deal show. Click **Settle**. See what's broken. 93 3. Pick a Flat-deal show. Click **Settle**. See what works. 94 4. Read `data/transcripts/*.md` and `data/ceo-memo.md`. 95 5. Look at `data/dispute-thread.md`. Then press **⌘K** and search "Coastal Spell" to find the matching show. 96 97 --- 98 99 ## How the data is shaped 100 101 Twenty-four months of synthetic operational data, designed to feel like a real venue: 102 103 | Table | Approx rows | What it represents | 104 |---|---|---| 105 | `shows` | ~540 | 24 months of shows. The app displays only past shows (more appear as days pass). | 106 | `artists` | 59 | Mix of recurring (A-tier, 4+ shows) and one-off (D-tier) acts | 107 | `agents` | 14 | Across WME, CAA, Wasserman, Paradigm, and independents | 108 | `deals` | ~540 | One per show. Mix is flat ~33%, vs ~33%, % of net ~24%, door ~5%, % of gross ~4% | 109 | `ticket_sales` | ~540 | One summary row per show, with realistic sell-through distributions | 110 | `comps` | ~1,900 | Comp tickets across 6 categories | 111 | `expenses` | ~2,900 | Sound, lights, hospitality, marketing, production, backline | 112 | `settlements` | ~540 | All shows have settlement data. Past shows display it; future shows hold it until their date arrives. | 113 114 A few things worth knowing: 115 116 **The deal `notes_freetext` field is the truth.** The structured fields (`guarantee_amount`, `percentage`, `bonuses_json`, `expense_cap`) are filled inconsistently. Mariana enters deals as prose because the structured fields don't model the actual deals well. This mismatch is part of the realism. 117 118 **Vs deals come in flavors.** About a third of Vs deals are "standard." The rest mix in walkout pots, tier ratchets, and vs-gross variants. The current in-app tool can't settle most of these. 119 120 **Settlements have a lifecycle.** The state machine runs draft → submitted → in_review → signed (or disputed) → revised → finalized → paid → voided. 121 122 **Recoups are categorized.** Settlement records carry a `recoups_json` field with line items in categories like `marketing`, `hospitality_overage`, `production_overage`. Each can be `agreed`, `disputed`, or `withdrawn`. 123 124 --- 125 126 ## A note before you start 127 128 Real venue data is messy. Fields drift over time. Prose contradicts structured values. Statuses don't always match the underlying reality. Patterns hide across many shows that look unremarkable in isolation. **What the UI shows you isn't always what the data says — and neither is necessarily what actually happened.** 129 130 We'd encourage you to read the data closely, query `data/greenroom.db` directly, and bring skepticism to anything that seems clean. The candidates we hire are the ones who notice that the surface-level view is incomplete. 131 132 --- 133 134 ## Where to look for context 135 136 ``` 137 data/ 138 ├── ceo-memo.md # Pri's Q4 memo: "winning on completeness, losing on craft" 139 ├── dispute-thread.md # The March 2025 marketing-recoup dispute, in full 140 ├── greenroom.db # SQLite database — pre-seeded, ready to go 141 └── transcripts/ 142 ├── mariana.md # 30-min interview with the booker 143 ├── diego.md # Tour manager perspective 144 ├── marcus.md # GM perspective 145 └── sarah-kim.md # Agent perspective (WME) 146 ``` 147 148 These aren't decorative. They contain signals the database deliberately doesn't capture — Mariana's frustrations, the agent's pet peeves, the things that escalate disputes. Mine them. 149 150 --- 151 152 ## File map 153 154 ``` 155 app/ 156 context/ # Candidate orientation page 157 shows/ # Show list with search + month grouping 158 shows/[id]/ # Show detail (concert poster-style header) 159 shows/[id]/settle/ # The settlement worksheet (hero number layout) 160 artists/ # Artist roster (card grid with genre dots) 161 reports/ # Aggregate metrics + craft gap analysis 162 icon.svg # Brand favicon 163 opengraph-image.tsx # Social share image 164 components/ 165 brand/logo.tsx # The Greenroom frequency-mark logomark / wordmark 166 command-palette/ # ⌘K global search (shows + artists) 167 ui/ # Buttons, badges, cards 168 layout/ 169 sidebar.tsx # Fixed sidebar with active nav state 170 nav-links.tsx # Client component for pathname-aware nav 171 lib/ 172 dealMath.ts # The settlement engine (deliberately incomplete) 173 queries.ts # Server-side data fetching (past shows only) 174 format.ts # Money + date helpers 175 db/ 176 schema.ts # All tables, commented 177 seed.ts # The 24-month synthetic seed 178 index.ts # libsql + Drizzle client 179 data/ # Markdown context + greenroom.db 180 ``` 181 182 --- 183 184 ## Tech stack 185 186 - **Next.js 16** (App Router) + **React 19** + **TypeScript** 187 - **Tailwind CSS 4** with shadcn-style component primitives 188 - **Drizzle ORM** + **libsql** (pure-JS SQLite — no native compile, no setup) 189 - **Fraunces** (variable serif, via `next/font/google`) for display headings 190 - **Geist Sans / Mono** (self-hosted via the `geist` package) for body + code 191 - **lucide-react** for icons, **date-fns** for dates 192 193 Everything is deliberately conventional. Use Cursor, Claude Code, or any other AI tool to navigate and modify the codebase — we expect you to. 194 195 --- 196 197 ## How to submit 198 199 When you're done: 200 201 1. **Push your branch.** `git add . && git commit -m "your message" && git push` 202 2. **Send the hiring contact:** 203 - The link to your forked repo 204 - Your 3–5 page PRD-quality memo (PDF, Notion, or Google Doc) 205 - A 5–10 minute Loom walking us through the prototype and memo together 206 207 --- 208 209 ## Troubleshooting 210 211 ### "Command not found: npm" or "node is not recognized" 212 213 Node.js isn't installed (or isn't on your PATH). Install from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org/), then restart your terminal. 214 215 ### "Port 3000 is already in use" 216 217 Something else is using port 3000. Two options: 218 219 **Stop the other thing first.** 220 - Mac/Linux: `lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill -9` 221 - Windows: `netstat -ano | findstr :3000` then `taskkill /PID /F` 222 223 **Or run on a different port:** 224 ```bash 225 npm run dev -- -p 3001 226 ``` 227 228 ### "Module not found" or weird build errors 229 230 Your `node_modules` is probably corrupt or incomplete. Reset it: 231 232 ```bash 233 rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json 234 npm install 235 ``` 236 237 ### The database looks empty, or you broke the data while exploring 238 239 Reset the database: 240 241 ```bash 242 npm run db:reset 243 ``` 244 245 This drops the SQLite file and regenerates 24 months of data. Takes ~5 seconds. Deterministic — same data every time. 246 247 ### Page looks ugly or buttons aren't visible 248 249 Hard-refresh your browser to clear the CSS cache: 250 - Mac: **⌘ + Shift + R** 251 - Windows/Linux: **Ctrl + Shift + R** 252 253 ### "I want to see what's actually in the database" 254 255 ```bash 256 npm run db:studio 257 ``` 258 259 Opens [Drizzle Studio](https://orm.drizzle.team/drizzle-studio/overview) at `local.drizzle.studio` — a visual table browser. You can also open `data/greenroom.db` with any SQLite client (e.g. [TablePlus](https://tableplus.com/), [DBeaver](https://dbeaver.io/), or `sqlite3` CLI). 260 261 ### Anything else 262 263 If you're stuck, email the hiring contact. We'd rather you ask than burn an hour fighting a setup issue. 264 265 --- 266 267 Welcome to The Crescent. 268

# Greenroom **Software for independent music venues.** This is the starter codebase for the Greenroom Applied AI PM case study.
--- You're looking at a working but mediocre product. It's enough to feel real, but every workflow has gaps. **Your job isn't to fix everything — it's to pick a slice and design it well.** See your case study brief for full instructions. ## Before you start You'll need: 1. **Node.js, version 20 or higher** — get it from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org/) (pick the LTS version). Verify with `node -v`. 2. **Git** — most computers have it. Verify with `git --version`. If not, install from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com/). 3. **A code editor.** [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/) is great. [Cursor](https://cursor.com/) is what we'd reach for if we were doing this case study. 4. **A GitHub account.** Free at [github.com](https://github.com/). If you're on Windows, run all the commands below in **Git Bash**, **PowerShell**, or **WSL** — not the legacy Command Prompt. ## Setup, step by step ### 1. Fork this repo to your own GitHub account Click the **Fork** button at the top right of [https://github.com/samay-cbh/greenroom-starter](https://github.com/samay-cbh/greenroom-starter). You'll get a copy under your own username. > _Why fork?_ A fork is your own copy of the repo. You'll commit your changes there, and submit your fork's URL when you're done. We can see your commit history that way. ### 2. Clone your fork to your computer ```bash git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/greenroom-starter cd greenroom-starter ``` (Replace `YOUR-USERNAME` with your actual GitHub username.) ### 3. Install dependencies ```bash npm install ``` This pulls down all the JavaScript packages the project needs. Takes about 60 seconds. You may see a few warnings — those are normal and safe to ignore. ### 4. Start the app ```bash npm run dev ``` You'll see something like: ``` ▲ Next.js 16.x - Local: http://localhost:3000 ✓ Ready in 1.2s ``` ### 5. Open it in your browser Go to **[http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000)**. You'll land on Mariana's home view at The Crescent. **Click "Where to start" in the sidebar** for an in-product orientation. > **Tip:** Press **⌘K** (Mac) or **Ctrl+K** (Windows/Linux) anywhere in the app to open the command palette — search across shows and artists instantly. --- ## What's running You're logged in automatically as **Mariana Reyes**, lead booker at The Crescent (650-cap, Nashville). The product has these surfaces: | Route | What it is | |---|---| | `/shows` | Mariana's home view. 24 months of completed shows, searchable and grouped by month. | | `/shows/[id]` | Show detail. Deal terms, artist info, ticket sales, expenses, comps. | | `/shows/[id]/settle` | The in-app settlement worksheet. **Try it on a few shows.** | | `/artists` | Roster of artists who've played the venue, bucketed by frequency. | | `/reports` | Aggregate metrics. The numbers Pri (the CEO) is watching. | | `/context` | Orientation for you, the candidate. Linked from the sidebar. | ### Recommended path your first time through 1. Open `/context` (the sidebar's "Where to start" link). 5-minute tour. 2. Then `/shows`. Pick a Vs-deal show. Click **Settle**. See what's broken. 3. Pick a Flat-deal show. Click **Settle**. See what works. 4. Read `data/transcripts/*.md` and `data/ceo-memo.md`. 5. Look at `data/dispute-thread.md`. Then press **⌘K** and search "Coastal Spell" to find the matching show. --- ## How the data is shaped Twenty-four months of synthetic operational data, designed to feel like a real venue: | Table | Approx rows | What it represents | |---|---|---| | `shows` | ~540 | 24 months of shows. The app displays only past shows (more appear as days pass). | | `artists` | 59 | Mix of recurring (A-tier, 4+ shows) and one-off (D-tier) acts | | `agents` | 14 | Across WME, CAA, Wasserman, Paradigm, and independents | | `deals` | ~540 | One per show. Mix is flat ~33%, vs ~33%, % of net ~24%, door ~5%, % of gross ~4% | | `ticket_sales` | ~540 | One summary row per show, with realistic sell-through distributions | | `comps` | ~1,900 | Comp tickets across 6 categories | | `expenses` | ~2,900 | Sound, lights, hospitality, marketing, production, backline | | `settlements` | ~540 | All shows have settlement data. Past shows display it; future shows hold it until their date arrives. | A few things worth knowing: **The deal `notes_freetext` field is the truth.** The structured fields (`guarantee_amount`, `percentage`, `bonuses_json`, `expense_cap`) are filled inconsistently. Mariana enters deals as prose because the structured fields don't model the actual deals well. This mismatch is part of the realism. **Vs deals come in flavors.** About a third of Vs deals are "standard." The rest mix in walkout pots, tier ratchets, and vs-gross variants. The current in-app tool can't settle most of these. **Settlements have a lifecycle.** The state machine runs draft → submitted → in_review → signed (or disputed) → revised → finalized → paid → voided. **Recoups are categorized.** Settlement records carry a `recoups_json` field with line items in categories like `marketing`, `hospitality_overage`, `production_overage`. Each can be `agreed`, `disputed`, or `withdrawn`. --- ## A note before you start Real venue data is messy. Fields drift over time. Prose contradicts structured values. Statuses don't always match the underlying reality. Patterns hide across many shows that look unremarkable in isolation. **What the UI shows you isn't always what the data says — and neither is necessarily what actually happened.** We'd encourage you to read the data closely, query `data/greenroom.db` directly, and bring skepticism to anything that seems clean. The candidates we hire are the ones who notice that the surface-level view is incomplete. --- ## Where to look for context ``` data/ ├── ceo-memo.md # Pri's Q4 memo: "winning on completeness, losing on craft" ├── dispute-thread.md # The March 2025 marketing-recoup dispute, in full ├── greenroom.db # SQLite database — pre-seeded, ready to go └── transcripts/ ├── mariana.md # 30-min interview with the booker ├── diego.md # Tour manager perspective ├── marcus.md # GM perspective └── sarah-kim.md # Agent perspective (WME) ``` These aren't decorative. They contain signals the database deliberately doesn't capture — Mariana's frustrations, the agent's pet peeves, the things that escalate disputes. Mine them. --- ## File map ``` app/ context/ # Candidate orientation page shows/ # Show list with search + month grouping shows/[id]/ # Show detail (concert poster-style header) shows/[id]/settle/ # The settlement worksheet (hero number layout) artists/ # Artist roster (card grid with genre dots) reports/ # Aggregate metrics + craft gap analysis icon.svg # Brand favicon opengraph-image.tsx # Social share image components/ brand/logo.tsx # The Greenroom frequency-mark logomark / wordmark command-palette/ # ⌘K global search (shows + artists) ui/ # Buttons, badges, cards layout/ sidebar.tsx # Fixed sidebar with active nav state nav-links.tsx # Client component for pathname-aware nav lib/ dealMath.ts # The settlement engine (deliberately incomplete) queries.ts # Server-side data fetching (past shows only) format.ts # Money + date helpers db/ schema.ts # All tables, commented seed.ts # The 24-month synthetic seed index.ts # libsql + Drizzle client data/ # Markdown context + greenroom.db ``` --- ## Tech stack - **Next.js 16** (App Router) + **React 19** + **TypeScript** - **Tailwind CSS 4** with shadcn-style component primitives - **Drizzle ORM** + **libsql** (pure-JS SQLite — no native compile, no setup) - **Fraunces** (variable serif, via `next/font/google`) for display headings - **Geist Sans / Mono** (self-hosted via the `geist` package) for body + code - **lucide-react** for icons, **date-fns** for dates Everything is deliberately conventional. Use Cursor, Claude Code, or any other AI tool to navigate and modify the codebase — we expect you to. --- ## How to submit When you're done: 1. **Push your branch.** `git add . && git commit -m "your message" && git push` 2. **Send the hiring contact:** - The link to your forked repo - Your 3–5 page PRD-quality memo (PDF, Notion, or Google Doc) - A 5–10 minute Loom walking us through the prototype and memo together --- ## Troubleshooting ### "Command not found: npm" or "node is not recognized" Node.js isn't installed (or isn't on your PATH). Install from [nodejs.org](https://nodejs.org/), then restart your terminal. ### "Port 3000 is already in use" Something else is using port 3000. Two options: **Stop the other thing first.** - Mac/Linux: `lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill -9` - Windows: `netstat -ano | findstr :3000` then `taskkill /PID /F` **Or run on a different port:** ```bash npm run dev -- -p 3001 ``` ### "Module not found" or weird build errors Your `node_modules` is probably corrupt or incomplete. Reset it: ```bash rm -rf node_modules package-lock.json npm install ``` ### The database looks empty, or you broke the data while exploring Reset the database: ```bash npm run db:reset ``` This drops the SQLite file and regenerates 24 months of data. Takes ~5 seconds. Deterministic — same data every time. ### Page looks ugly or buttons aren't visible Hard-refresh your browser to clear the CSS cache: - Mac: **⌘ + Shift + R** - Windows/Linux: **Ctrl + Shift + R** ### "I want to see what's actually in the database" ```bash npm run db:studio ``` Opens [Drizzle Studio](https://orm.drizzle.team/drizzle-studio/overview) at `local.drizzle.studio` — a visual table browser. You can also open `data/greenroom.db` with any SQLite client (e.g. [TablePlus](https://tableplus.com/), [DBeaver](https://dbeaver.io/), or `sqlite3` CLI). ### Anything else If you're stuck, email the hiring contact. We'd rather you ask than burn an hour fighting a setup issue. --- Welcome to The Crescent.

1	/**
2	 * Greenroom database schema.
3	 *
4	 * The data model is deliberately simple but realistic enough to support
5	 * the settlement workflows. Mariana (the booker at The Crescent) is the
6	 * primary user. Other personas (tour managers, agents, the GM) appear
7	 * in the data but don't have UI here.
8	 */
9	
10	import { sqliteTable, text, integer, real } from "drizzle-orm/sqlite-core";
11	
12	// -------- Users (operator accounts at the venue) --------
13	
14	export const users = sqliteTable("users", {
15	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
16	  name: text("name").notNull(),
17	  email: text("email").notNull().unique(),
18	  role: text("role", {
19	    enum: ["booker", "gm", "production", "box_office"],
20	  }).notNull(),
21	  venueId: text("venue_id").notNull(),
22	});
23	
24	// -------- Venues --------
25	
26	export const venues = sqliteTable("venues", {
27	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
28	  name: text("name").notNull(),
29	  capacity: integer("capacity").notNull(),
30	  city: text("city").notNull(),
31	  state: text("state").notNull(),
32	});
33	
34	// -------- Agencies & Agents --------
35	
36	export const agencies = sqliteTable("agencies", {
37	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
38	  name: text("name").notNull(),
39	});
40	
41	export const agents = sqliteTable("agents", {
42	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
43	  name: text("name").notNull(),
44	  agencyId: text("agency_id").references(() => agencies.id),
45	  email: text("email").notNull(),
46	  phone: text("phone"),
47	  preferencesNotes: text("preferences_notes"),
48	});
49	
50	// -------- Artists --------
51	
52	export const artists = sqliteTable("artists", {
53	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
54	  name: text("name").notNull(),
55	  agentId: text("agent_id").references(() => agents.id),
56	  managerEmail: text("manager_email"),
57	  genre: text("genre"),
58	  priorShowCount: integer("prior_show_count").notNull().default(0),
59	});
60	
61	// -------- Shows --------
62	
63	export const shows = sqliteTable("shows", {
64	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
65	  venueId: text("venue_id")
66	    .notNull()
67	    .references(() => venues.id),
68	  artistId: text("artist_id")
69	    .notNull()
70	    .references(() => artists.id),
71	  date: text("date").notNull(),
72	  status: text("status", {
73	    enum: ["booked", "advanced", "day_of", "settled", "closed"],
74	  })
75	    .notNull()
76	    .default("booked"),
77	  doorsTime: text("doors_time"),
78	  setTime: text("set_time"),
79	  openerArtistId: text("opener_artist_id").references(() => artists.id),
80	  roomConfig: text("room_config", { enum: ["standing", "seated", "mixed"] })
81	    .notNull()
82	    .default("standing"),
83	  internalNotes: text("internal_notes"),
84	  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
85	});
86	
87	// -------- Deals --------
88	
89	/**
90	 * One deal per show. The structure here is deliberately split:
91	 *
92	 *  - `dealType` and the structured fields (guarantee, percentage, etc.)
93	 *    are what the in-app settlement tool reads.
94	 *  - `dealNotesFreetext` is what Mariana actually trusts.
95	 *  - `bonusesJson` exists in the schema but the in-app tool doesn't read it.
96	 *    It's been there since 2023, originally added by a now-departed PM. About
97	 *    half the deals that have bonus structures fill it in; the other half
98	 *    leave it empty and put the bonuses in the prose only. That mismatch
99	 *    is one of the case-study seams.
100	 *
101	 * bonusesJson schema (when present):
102	 *   [
103	 *     { type: "gross_threshold", label: string, threshold: number, amount: number, stacks?: boolean },
104	 *     { type: "sellout", label: string, amount: number },
105	 *     { type: "attendance_threshold", label: string, threshold: number, amount: number },
106	 *     { type: "tier_ratchet", label: string, tiers: [{ from, to|null, percentage }] }
107	 *   ]
108	 */
109	export const deals = sqliteTable("deals", {
110	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
111	  showId: text("show_id")
112	    .notNull()
113	    .unique()
114	    .references(() => shows.id),
115	
116	  dealType: text("deal_type", {
117	    enum: ["flat", "percentage_of_gross", "percentage_of_net", "vs", "door"],
118	  }).notNull(),
119	  guaranteeAmount: real("guarantee_amount"),
120	  percentage: real("percentage"),
121	  percentageBasis: text("percentage_basis", { enum: ["gross", "net"] }),
122	  expenseCap: real("expense_cap"),
123	  hospitalityCap: real("hospitality_cap"),
124	
125	  bonusesJson: text("bonuses_json"),
126	  dealNotesFreetext: text("deal_notes_freetext"),
127	
128	  createdAt: integer("created_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
129	});
130	
131	// -------- Ticket sales --------
132	
133	export const ticketSales = sqliteTable("ticket_sales", {
134	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
135	  showId: text("show_id")
136	    .notNull()
137	    .references(() => shows.id),
138	  qty: integer("qty").notNull(),
139	  gross: real("gross").notNull(),
140	  fees: real("fees").notNull(),
141	  capturedAt: integer("captured_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
142	});
143	
144	// -------- Comps --------
145	
146	/**
147	 * Comp tickets given away. A real source of dispute — agents sometimes argue
148	 * that certain comps "should have counted toward the gross" the artist's % is
149	 * calculated against. Most comps don't count, but the rules vary by category
150	 * and sometimes by deal.
151	 *
152	 * Stored as one row per category per show (aggregated count, not per-ticket).
153	 */
154	export const comps = sqliteTable("comps", {
155	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
156	  showId: text("show_id")
157	    .notNull()
158	    .references(() => shows.id),
159	  category: text("category", {
160	    enum: [
161	      "artist_gl", // artist guest list
162	      "label", // label/management
163	      "press", // journalists, photographers
164	      "venue_staff", // venue staff and friends
165	      "sponsor", // sponsor reps
166	      "promo", // radio giveaways, 2-for-1s
167	      "other",
168	    ],
169	  }).notNull(),
170	  count: integer("count").notNull(),
171	  faceValue: real("face_value").notNull(),
172	  // Whether these comps count toward gross box office for settlement purposes.
173	  // Most categories don't, but the rules are inconsistent across deals.
174	  countsTowardGross: integer("counts_toward_gross", { mode: "boolean" })
175	    .notNull()
176	    .default(false),
177	  notes: text("notes"),
178	});
179	
180	// -------- Expenses --------
181	
182	export const expenses = sqliteTable("expenses", {
183	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
184	  showId: text("show_id")
185	    .notNull()
186	    .references(() => shows.id),
187	  category: text("category", {
188	    enum: [
189	      "production",
190	      "sound",
191	      "lights",
192	      "hospitality",
193	      "marketing",
194	      "backline",
195	      "security",
196	      "other",
197	    ],
198	  }).notNull(),
199	  amount: real("amount").notNull(),
200	  description: text("description"),
201	  approved: integer("approved", { mode: "boolean" }).notNull().default(true),
202	  absorbedByVenue: integer("absorbed_by_venue", { mode: "boolean" })
203	    .notNull()
204	    .default(false),
205	  enteredByUserId: text("entered_by_user_id").references(() => users.id),
206	  enteredAt: integer("entered_at", { mode: "timestamp" }).notNull(),
207	});
208	
209	// -------- Settlements --------
210	
211	/**
212	 * The post-show financial reconciliation. One per show.
213	 *
214	 * Settlement is a multi-party state machine: the venue calculates a number,
215	 * sends it to the artist's tour manager / agent for review, they sign or
216	 * dispute, revisions happen, eventually a final number is agreed and money
217	 * moves.
218	 *
219	 * Stage timestamps below capture each transition. `status` is the current
220	 * stage. Most past shows are `paid`; recent ones are still in-flight.
221	 *
222	 * recoupsJson schema (when present):
223	 *   [
224	 *     { id, category: "marketing"|"hospitality_overage"|"production_overage"|"prior_advance"|"damages"|"other",
225	 *       label: string, amount: number, status: "agreed"|"disputed"|"withdrawn" }
226	 *   ]
227	 *
228	 * Recoups are venue costs that come "off the top" before artist payment.
229	 * They differ from regular expenses in two ways:
230	 *   1. They're disputed more often — the deal email language about recoups
231	 *      is frequently ambiguous (see the Coastal Spell dispute).
232	 *   2. They have their own lifecycle independent of the rest of the
233	 *      settlement — a recoup can be disputed even after the rest of the
234	 *      math is signed.
235	 */
236	export const settlements = sqliteTable("settlements", {
237	  id: text("id").primaryKey(),
238	  showId: text("show_id")
239	    .notNull()
240	    .unique()
241	    .references(() => shows.id),
242	
243	  status: text("status", {
244	    enum: [
245	      "draft", // booker is still doing math
246	      "submitted", // sent to artist team
247	      "in_review", // artist team has opened it
248	      "signed", // both parties agree
249	      "disputed", // line items contested
250	      "revised", // venue sent a revision after dispute
251	      "finalized", // signed after revision
252	      "paid", // money has moved
253	      "voided", // show cancelled or settlement scrapped
254	    ],
255	  })
256	    .notNull()
257	    .default("draft"),
258

1	/**
2	 * Settlement lifecycle helpers.
3	 *
4	 * The settlement state machine:
5	 *
6	 *   draft → submitted → in_review → signed → finalized → paid
7	 *                              \→ disputed → revised ↗
8	 *
9	 *   voided is a terminal off-ramp (cancelled show / scrapped settlement)
10	 */
11	
12	import type { Settlement, SettlementStage } from "@/db/schema";
13	
14	export const STAGE_ORDER: SettlementStage[] = [
15	  "draft",
16	  "submitted",
17	  "in_review",
18	  "signed",
19	  "disputed",
20	  "revised",
21	  "finalized",
22	  "paid",
23	];
24	
25	export const STAGE_LABELS: Record<SettlementStage, string> = {
26	  draft: "Draft",
27	  submitted: "Submitted",
28	  in_review: "In review",
29	  signed: "Signed",
30	  disputed: "Disputed",
31	  revised: "Revised",
32	  finalized: "Finalized",
33	  paid: "Paid",
34	  voided: "Voided",
35	};
36	
37	export const STAGE_DESCRIPTIONS: Record<SettlementStage, string> = {
38	  draft:
39	    "Mariana is still entering numbers. Not yet sent to the artist team.",
40	  submitted:
41	    "Sent to the artist's tour manager. Waiting for them to start review.",
42	  in_review:
43	    "The artist team has opened the settlement and is reviewing line items.",
44	  signed:
45	    "The artist team has approved the math. Money has not yet moved.",
46	  disputed:
47	    "At least one line item is contested. The settlement is on hold.",
48	  revised:
49	    "A revised version has been sent in response to a dispute. Awaiting acceptance.",
50	  finalized:
51	    "The revised settlement has been agreed. Awaiting payment.",
52	  paid: "Money has moved. The settlement is closed.",
53	  voided:
54	    "The settlement was scrapped (cancelled show, force majeure, etc.).",
55	};
56	
57	/** Visual style hints for each stage — used by the UI. */
58	export const STAGE_TONES: Record<
59	  SettlementStage,
60	  "neutral" | "active" | "complete" | "warning" | "danger"
61	> = {
62	  draft: "active",
63	  submitted: "active",
64	  in_review: "active",
65	  signed: "complete",
66	  disputed: "warning",
67	  revised: "warning",
68	  finalized: "complete",
69	  paid: "complete",
70	  voided: "danger",
71	};
72	
73	/** What stage transitions are available from a given stage? */
74	export function nextStages(stage: SettlementStage): SettlementStage[] {
75	  switch (stage) {
76	    case "draft":
77	      return ["submitted", "voided"];
78	    case "submitted":
79	      return ["in_review", "voided"];
80	    case "in_review":
81	      return ["signed", "disputed"];
82	    case "signed":
83	      return ["paid"];
84	    case "disputed":
85	      return ["revised", "voided"];
86	    case "revised":
87	      return ["finalized", "disputed"];
88	    case "finalized":
89	      return ["paid"];
90	    case "paid":
91	      return [];
92	    case "voided":
93	      return [];
94	  }
95	}
96	
97	/** Returns the stages this settlement has been through, in order, with timestamps. */
98	export function stageHistory(s: Settlement) {
99	  const history: { stage: SettlementStage; at: Date }[] = [];
100	  if (s.draftedAt) history.push({ stage: "draft", at: s.draftedAt });
101	  if (s.submittedAt) history.push({ stage: "submitted", at: s.submittedAt });
102	  if (s.reviewStartedAt)
103	    history.push({ stage: "in_review", at: s.reviewStartedAt });
104	  if (s.disputedAt) history.push({ stage: "disputed", at: s.disputedAt });
105	  if (s.revisedAt) history.push({ stage: "revised", at: s.revisedAt });
106	  if (s.signedAt) history.push({ stage: "signed", at: s.signedAt });
107	  if (s.finalizedAt) history.push({ stage: "finalized", at: s.finalizedAt });
108	  if (s.paidAt) history.push({ stage: "paid", at: s.paidAt });
109	  return history.sort((a, b) => a.at.getTime() - b.at.getTime());
110	}

1	/**
2	 * Greenroom 24-month synthetic seed.
3	 *
4	 * Generates ~540 shows across 24 months at The Crescent, with realistic:
5	 *   - artist tiers (draw size distributions)
6	 *   - deal type mix (flat 25%, vs 50%, percentage_of_net 15%, door 5%, percentage_of_gross 5%)
7	 *   - bonus structures (~30% of vs / % of net deals; mix of gross thresholds, sellout bonuses,
8	 *     attendance thresholds, and tier ratchets — some duplicated in prose, some only in prose)
9	 *   - sell-through variance
10	 *   - comps per show (artist GL, label, press, venue staff, sponsor, promo)
11	 *   - expense breakdowns
12	 *   - past settlements with deal-aware math
13	 *   - settlement lifecycle stages (draft → submitted → in_review → signed/disputed → revised → finalized → paid)
14	 *   - recoup line items on ~30% of past settlements (some agreed, some disputed)
15	 *
16	 * Specific narrative shows are injected by hand:
17	 *   - The Coastal Spell / WME dispute (March 14, 2025) referenced in
18	 *     data/dispute-thread.md
19	 *
20	 * Run via: npm run db:seed
21	 */

42	// -------- Deterministic RNG --------
43	function makeRng(seed: number) {
44	  let t = seed >>> 0;
45	  return () => {
46	    t = (t + 0x6d2b79f5) >>> 0;
47	    let r = Math.imul(t ^ (t >>> 15), 1 | t);
48	    r = (r + Math.imul(r ^ (r >>> 7), 61 | r)) ^ r;
49	    return ((r ^ (r >>> 14)) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
50	  };
51	}
52	const rng = makeRng(42);
53	const rnd = () => rng();
54	const rndInt = (min: number, max: number) =>
55	  Math.floor(rnd() * (max - min + 1)) + min;
56	const choose = <T>(arr: T[]): T => arr[Math.floor(rnd() * arr.length)];
57	const weighted = <T>(items: { value: T; weight: number }[]): T => {
58	  const total = items.reduce((s, i) => s + i.weight, 0);
59	  let r = rnd() * total;
60	  for (const i of items) {
61	    r -= i.weight;
62	    if (r <= 0) return i.value;
63	  }
64	  return items[items.length - 1].value;
65	};

# Q4 All-Hands: Where We Are, And Where We're Not

**From:** Pri Shankar
**To:** Greenroom team
**Date:** January 2026

Team,

We just closed our best quarter ever on the topline. ARR is at $8.1M, up from $6.4M when we kicked off the year. We added 73 net new venues, mostly in Texas, Colorado, and the Pacific Northwest. Net dollar retention is 116%. By every dashboard metric the board sees, we're winning.

I want to spend this memo talking about why I'm not satisfied.

When I do my customer calls — and I do five of them a week, usually with bookers, sometimes with GMs — there's a pattern. They love us. They specifically love that they can run their whole operation on Greenroom. Calendar, advances, marketing, settlements, sponsor reporting, fan database — all in one place. The pitch works. Three years in, the comprehensive-platform thesis was right.

But every single one of them says some version of the same thing next: "the individual features are kinda just okay."

The booker at a venue in Portland told me last week that she does all her settlements in a Google Sheet because our settlement tool doesn't handle vs deals. The talent buyer in Asheville said our advance workspace is "better than email but worse than my old PDF system." The GM at a Boston venue, who I know likes us a lot, told me he doesn't trust our show-margin reports enough to share them with his lender.

This is fixable, but I want us to be honest about what it means.

**We are winning on completeness and losing on craft.**

I think this is the most important sentence I'll write this quarter. Our customers love us in spite of our individual features, not because of them. Some of them love us enough to put up with it. The ones who leave us are the ones who hit a feature they care about and find it half-built.

The market that's going to survive the next decade is independent venues with the strongest artist relationships. Live Nation is buying every venue they can. AEG is right behind them. The independents who hold on are going to be the ones agents trust to settle cleanly, fill the room consistently, and treat artists well. Our job is to be the operational backbone of that.

We are not yet good enough at the most important moment of those relationships. Specifically:

**Settlement.** This is where we are most clearly failing. Eighteen percent of our customer base actively uses the in-app settlement tool. The other 82% — including most of our largest accounts — default to spreadsheets. The math gets done somewhere else. The 2am ritual happens somewhere else. The trust between the venue and the artist is built somewhere else. We're not in that conversation. We have to be.

**Advance documents.** Every show generates a five-to-fifteen-page PDF advance from the touring artist. Our workflow is that the booker manually transcribes the relevant bits into our free-text fields and forwards pages by email. We've taken a problem that should be a structured handoff and made it slightly worse than email.

**Sponsor reporting.** Independent venues live or die on sponsor revenue. Bookers spend 6-8 hours per sponsor per quarter manually pulling data out of our system and three other systems to build a Powerpoint. We could be helping. We're not.

I'm not asking the team to fix all three of these in Q1. I'm asking us to pick where to start, go deep, and be honest about the trade-offs we're making.

For Q1, I want our craft bet to be **settlement.** Two reasons. One: it's the most trust-critical moment in the relationship between our customers and their customers' customers. Two: 82% of our base is going around our product to do this. That's not a feature gap. That's an existential signal.

Anil and the product team will be opening up case studies for senior PM hires this quarter, and the work they ship will inform what we build next. If you have strong opinions about what good settlement looks like, talk to them.

# Greenroom Starter — Repository Overview

A field guide to the [greenroom-starter](.) codebase: what it is, how it's built, and where the important content lives.

---

## 1. What this project is

**Greenroom** bills itself as "software for independent music venues" — calendar, advancing, marketing, settlements, sponsor reporting, fan database, all in one place. This particular repo is the **starter codebase for the Greenroom Applied AI PM case study**: a deliberately working-but-mediocre product seeded with 24 months of synthetic operational data for **The Crescent**, a 650-cap venue in Nashville.

The candidate (you) is auto-logged in as **Mariana Reyes**, lead booker. Per the [README](README.md): _"Your job isn't to fix everything — it's to pick a slice and design it well."_ The intended target slice is **settlement**, which the CEO memo flags as the company's biggest craft gap.

---

## 2. Tech stack

Pulled from [package.json](package.json):

| Concern | Choice |
|---|---|
| Framework | **Next.js 16.2.6** (App Router) |
| UI runtime | **React 19.2.4** |
| Language | **TypeScript 5** (strict, `@/*` path alias → repo root) |
| Styling | **Tailwind CSS 4** (`@theme` block in `globals.css`, no `tailwind.config`), shadcn-style primitives |
| DB | **libsql** (pure-JS SQLite) at `file:./data/greenroom.db` |
| ORM | **Drizzle ORM 0.45** + drizzle-kit 0.31 |
| Fonts | **Fraunces** (display serif), **Geist Sans/Mono** (body/code) |
| Icons | **lucide-react** |
| Dates | **date-fns 4** |
| Component utilities | `class-variance-authority`, `clsx`, `tailwind-merge` |

Scripts: `npm run dev | build | start | lint | db:push | db:seed | db:reset | db:studio`.

The repo is committed with `data/greenroom.db` checked in, so clone-and-run works without an initial seed.

---

## 3. Top-level layout

```
greenroom-starter/
├── app/                 # Next.js App Router routes
├── components/          # React components (brand, layout, ui, command-palette)
```

### Client — [db/index.ts](db/index.ts)
libsql client pointing at `process.env.DATABASE_URL ?? "file:./data/greenroom.db"`, wrapped in Drizzle and re-exported as `db`. Migrations live in [db/migrations/](db/migrations/) (`0000_initial.sql`, `0000_awesome_juggernaut.sql`, plus `meta/_journal.json` + snapshot).

### Schema — [db/schema.ts](db/schema.ts)
Eleven tables, all SQLite. Heavily commented; the comments are themselves part of the case-study substrate.

| Table | Notes |
|---|---|
| `users` | Operator accounts (`booker` / `gm` / `production` / `box_office`). |
| `venues` | The Crescent + future-proofing. |
| `agencies`, `agents` | WME / CAA / Wasserman / Paradigm / Independent. Agents carry `preferencesNotes` (pet peeves, behavior hints). |
| `artists` | 59 acts. `priorShowCount` denormalized. |
| `shows` | ~540 rows. Status enum: `booked` → `advanced` → `day_of` → `settled` → `closed`. Carries `internalNotes` (Mariana's prose). |
| `deals` | **The seam.** Structured fields (`guaranteeAmount`, `percentage`, `expenseCap`, `hospitalityCap`) coexist with `bonusesJson` and `dealNotesFreetext`. | `ticketSales` | One summary row per show (`qty`, `gross`, `fees`, `capturedAt`). |
| `comps` | One row per category per show. 7 categories; `countsTowardGross` boolean — a real source of dispute. |
| `expenses` | 8 categories (production / sound / lights / hospitality / marketing / backline / security / other). `absorbedByVenue` toggles whether passed through. |

Type exports at the bottom: `User`, `Venue`, `Agency`, `Agent`, `Artist`, `Show`, `Deal`, `TicketSale`, `Comp`, `Expense`, `Settlement`, plus the discriminated `Bonus` union, `Recoup`, and `SettlementStage`.

### Seed — [db/seed.ts](db/seed.ts) _(~1,477 lines)_
Deterministic (seeded RNG, `seed = 42`) generation of 24 months of data anchored on `TODAY`:
- Artist tiers A–D with draw distributions.
- Deal-type mix ~ flat 25% / vs 50% / % of net 15% / door 5% / % of gross 5%.
- Bonus structures on ~30% of vs / % of net deals, some duplicated in prose, some prose-only.
- Sell-through variance, comps by category, expenses.
- Settlement lifecycle stages with realistic stage timestamps.
- Recoups on ~30% of past settlements.
- **One hand-injected narrative show**: Coastal Spell, March 14 2025, the WME/Daniel Hwang dispute referenced verbatim in [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md). Deep-linked from `/context` as `/shows/show_coastal_spell_dispute`.

---

## 7. The `lib/` directory

### [lib/queries.ts](lib/queries.ts)
Server-only data fetching. Three exports:
- `getAllShows()` — past shows only (`shows.date <= todayDateString()`); joins artist, agent, deal, settlement.
- `getShowById(id)` — joins everything + parallel-fetches ticket sales, expenses, comps; safely parses `recoupsJson`.
- `getAllArtists()` — left-joined with show counts + last-show date.
- `getReports()` — aggregates for the reports page: deal-type counts, in-app-tool usage rate (defined as `flat + percentage_of_gross` over all deals), settlement-status histogram, disputed rate, gross/paid-to-artist totals, recoup totals, comps by category.

The `// past shows only` filter (`lte(shows.date, todayDateString())`) is the time-gate referenced in recent commits — future shows exist in the DB but are hidden in the query layer.

### [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts)
**The deliberately incomplete settlement engine.** Top-of-file comment is essential reading:
- Handles `flat` and `percentage_of_gross` end-to-end (with structured bonuses).
- Returns `{ supported: false }` for `vs`, `percentage_of_net`, `door` — the UI's empty state.
- Reads only `bonusesJson`; bonuses that live only in `dealNotesFreetext` are invisible.
- `applyBonuses()` evaluates `gross_threshold`, `sellout` (≥95% capacity), `attendance_threshold`; explicitly punts on `tier_ratchet` ("needs vs-deal or % of net support — not yet handled").

### [lib/settlementStage.ts](lib/settlementStage.ts)
Settlement-state-machine helpers: `STAGE_ORDER`, `STAGE_LABELS`, `STAGE_DESCRIPTIONS`, `STAGE_TONES` (neutral / active / complete / warning / danger), `nextStages(stage)`, and `stageHistory(s)` which builds an ordered timeline from the timestamp columns.

### [lib/format.ts](lib/format.ts)
`formatMoney`, `formatMoneyCompact`, `formatShowDate` ("Fri, May 7"), `formatShowDateFull`, `formatShowMonth`, `relativeShowDate` ("in 5 days" / "8 days ago").

### [lib/utils.ts](lib/utils.ts)
The conventional `cn()` (clsx + tailwind-merge).

---

## 8. Design system & visual language

[app/globals.css](app/globals.css) is the source of truth. Tailwind 4 `@theme` block defines:
- **Canvas** — `#faf7f0` / `#fdfbf6` (warm cream, paper-feel)
- **Brand** — emerald scale (`brand-50` → `brand-900`), primary `brand-700` (#047857)
- **Ink** — warm gray scale (`ink-50` → `ink-900`), text default `ink-900` (#1a1814)
- **Amber / Rose / Sky** — accent scales (50/100/200/700/800)
- Body has two fixed `::before`/`::after` overlays: radial brand-tinted gradients + a near-invisible SVG noise texture for paper grain.
- Utility classes: `.tabular` (tabular nums), `.font-display` (Fraunces), `.eyebrow` (11px uppercase brand-700 caption), `.bg-gradient-brand`, `.bg-gradient-paper`.
- `*:focus-visible` ringed in `brand-700`.

The product reaches consistently for: Fraunces display headlines (52–72px, tight tracking, optical sizing); compact tabular monospace for money; small `eyebrow` labels above numbers; status accent bars rather than full backgrounds.

---

## 9. The `data/` directory — narrative substrate

The markdown files here are **not decorative**. They carry signals the database deliberately doesn't capture.

| File | What it is |
|---|---|
| [data/greenroom.db](data/greenroom.db) | The seeded SQLite database, committed to git. |
| [data/ceo-memo.md](data/ceo-memo.md) | Pri Shankar's Q4 all-hands. The strategic frame: "winning on completeness, losing on craft." Explicitly names settlement as the Q1 craft bet (18% in-app use, 82% spreadsheet). |
| [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md) | Full email thread (Daniel Hwang @ WME ↔ Mariana) on the Coastal Spell March 14 2025 settlement. Centers on a `$900` marketing recoup whose treatment in the deal email is ambiguous — was it "off gross" or part of the `$2,500` expense cap? The dispute resolves at `$720` against The Crescent. The matching show is hand-seeded as `show_coastal_spell_dispute`. |
| [data/transcripts/mariana.md](data/transcripts/mariana.md) | 30-min interview with the booker. Pet peeves, the 2am settlement ritual. |
| [data/transcripts/diego.md](data/transcripts/diego.md) | Tour manager perspective. |
| [data/transcripts/marcus.md](data/transcripts/marcus.md) | GM perspective. |
| [data/transcripts/sarah-kim.md](data/transcripts/sarah-kim.md) | WME agent perspective. |

---

## 10. Configuration & build

- [tsconfig.json](tsconfig.json) — strict, ES2017 target, `module: esnext`, `moduleResolution: bundler`, `jsx: react-jsx`, Next plugin, `@/*` → `./*`.
- [next.config.ts](next.config.ts) — empty default `NextConfig`.
- [eslint.config.mjs](eslint.config.mjs) — composes `eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals` + `/typescript`, re-applies the default Next ignores explicitly.
- [drizzle.config.ts](drizzle.config.ts) — schema at `./db/schema.ts`, out at `./db/migrations`, sqlite dialect, `DATABASE_URL` overridable.
- [postcss.config.mjs](postcss.config.mjs) — `@tailwindcss/postcss` only.
- [.gitignore](.gitignore) — standard Next.js, plus `.env*`, `.vercel`, `*.tsbuildinfo`, `next-env.d.ts`. **Note:** `data/greenroom.db` is intentionally _not_ ignored.

---

## 11. The "seams" the case study points at

Reading the schema comments, `dealMath.ts`, the CEO memo, and the dispute thread together, the planted gaps are:

1. **Structured ↔ prose mismatch in deals.** `bonusesJson` exists; `dealNotesFreetext` is what's trusted. The in-app engine only reads the former.
2. **Deal-type coverage.** ~82% of deal types (vs / % of net / door) fall through to a "not supported" empty state.
3. **Recoups are their own lifecycle.** Disputed recoups can persist after the rest of the settlement is signed; recoup-line ambiguity is the single biggest dispute driver (per the Coastal Spell thread).
4. **Comps that count toward gross** vary per deal — another quiet source of friction.
5. **Settlement state machine** has 9 statuses; the UI surfaces them but does nothing to help the user move between them.
6. **Time gate.** Future shows live in the DB but the query layer hides them — recent commit history (`5105d5e`, `8692708`, `d910368`) shows this was deliberately tightened.

---

## 12. Suggested entry points when reading

If you're new to this repo, read in roughly this order:

1. [README.md](README.md) — quickstart + file map (you've likely already read it).
2. [data/ceo-memo.md](data/ceo-memo.md) — strategic frame.
3. [db/schema.ts](db/schema.ts) — the data model, comments and all.
4. [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts) — top comment + body. This is the seam.
5. [app/shows/[id]/settle/page.tsx](app/shows/%5Bid%5D/settle/page.tsx) — how the seam surfaces to the user.
6. [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md) — what the seam costs.
7. [data/transcripts/](data/transcripts/) — what users say about it.
8. [lib/queries.ts](lib/queries.ts) and [app/reports/page.tsx](app/reports/page.tsx) — how the org sees it in aggregate.

- `getAllArtists()` — left-joined with show counts + last-show date.
- `getReports()` — aggregates for the reports page: deal-type counts, in-app-tool usage rate (defined as `flat + percentage_of_gross` over all deals), settlement-status histogram, disputed rate, gross/paid-to-artist totals, recoup totals, comps by category.

The `// past shows only` filter (`lte(shows.date, todayDateString())`) is the time-gate referenced in recent commits — future shows exist in the DB but are hidden in the query layer.

### [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts)
**The deliberately incomplete settlement engine.** Top-of-file comment is essential reading:
- Handles `flat` and `percentage_of_gross` end-to-end (with structured bonuses).
- Returns `{ supported: false }` for `vs`, `percentage_of_net`, `door` — the UI's empty state.
- Reads only `bonusesJson`; bonuses that live only in `dealNotesFreetext` are invisible.
- `applyBonuses()` evaluates `gross_threshold`, `sellout` (≥95% capacity), `attendance_threshold`; explicitly punts on `tier_ratchet` ("needs vs-deal or % of net support — not yet handled").

### [lib/settlementStage.ts](lib/settlementStage.ts)
Settlement-state-machine helpers: `STAGE_ORDER`, `STAGE_LABELS`, `STAGE_DESCRIPTIONS`, `STAGE_TONES` (neutral / active / complete / warning / danger), `nextStages(stage)`, and `stageHistory(s)` which builds an ordered timeline from the timestamp columns.

### [lib/format.ts](lib/format.ts)
`formatMoney`, `formatMoneyCompact`, `formatShowDate` ("Fri, May 7"), `formatShowDateFull`, `formatShowMonth`, `relativeShowDate` ("in 5 days" / "8 days ago").

### [lib/utils.ts](lib/utils.ts)
The conventional `cn()` (clsx + tailwind-merge).

---

## 8. Design system & visual language

[app/globals.css](app/globals.css) is the source of truth. Tailwind 4 `@theme` block defines:
- **Canvas** — `#faf7f0` / `#fdfbf6` (warm cream, paper-feel)
- **Brand** — emerald scale (`brand-50` → `brand-900`), primary `brand-700` (#047857)
- **Ink** — warm gray scale (`ink-50` → `ink-900`), text default `ink-900` (#1a1814)
- **Amber / Rose / Sky** — accent scales (50/100/200/700/800)
- Body has two fixed `::before`/`::after` overlays: radial brand-tinted gradients + a near-invisible SVG noise texture for paper grain.
- Utility classes: `.tabular` (tabular nums), `.font-display` (Fraunces), `.eyebrow` (11px uppercase brand-700 caption), `.bg-gradient-brand`, `.bg-gradient-paper`.
- `*:focus-visible` ringed in `brand-700`.

The product reaches consistently for: Fraunces display headlines (52–72px, tight tracking, optical sizing); compact tabular monospace for money; small `eyebrow` labels above numbers; status accent bars rather than full backgrounds.

---

## 9. The `data/` directory — narrative substrate

The markdown files here are **not decorative**. They carry signals the database deliberately doesn't capture.

| File | What it is |
|---|---|
| [data/greenroom.db](data/greenroom.db) | The seeded SQLite database, committed to git. |
| [data/ceo-memo.md](data/ceo-memo.md) | Pri Shankar's Q4 all-hands. The strategic frame: "winning on completeness, losing on craft." Explicitly names settlement as the Q1 craft bet (18% in-app use, 82% spreadsheet). |
| [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md) | Full email thread (Daniel Hwang @ WME ↔ Mariana) on the Coastal Spell March 14 2025 settlement. Centers on a `$900` marketing recoup whose treatment in the deal email is ambiguous — was it "off gross" or part of the `$2,500` expense cap? The dispute resolves at `$720` against The Crescent. The matching show is hand-seeded as `show_coastal_spell_dispute`. |
| [data/transcripts/mariana.md](data/transcripts/mariana.md) | 30-min interview with the booker. Pet peeves, the 2am settlement ritual. |
| [data/transcripts/diego.md](data/transcripts/diego.md) | Tour manager perspective. |
| [data/transcripts/marcus.md](data/transcripts/marcus.md) | GM perspective. |
| [data/transcripts/sarah-kim.md](data/transcripts/sarah-kim.md) | WME agent perspective. |

---

## 10. Configuration & build

- [tsconfig.json](tsconfig.json) — strict, ES2017 target, `module: esnext`, `moduleResolution: bundler`, `jsx: react-jsx`, Next plugin, `@/*` → `./*`.
- [next.config.ts](next.config.ts) — empty default `NextConfig`.
- [eslint.config.mjs](eslint.config.mjs) — composes `eslint-config-next/core-web-vitals` + `/typescript`, re-applies the default Next ignores explicitly.
- [drizzle.config.ts](drizzle.config.ts) — schema at `./db/schema.ts`, out at `./db/migrations`, sqlite dialect, `DATABASE_URL` overridable.
- [postcss.config.mjs](postcss.config.mjs) — `@tailwindcss/postcss` only.
- [.gitignore](.gitignore) — standard Next.js, plus `.env*`, `.vercel`, `*.tsbuildinfo`, `next-env.d.ts`. **Note:** `data/greenroom.db` is intentionally _not_ ignored.

---

## 11. The "seams" the case study points at

Reading the schema comments, `dealMath.ts`, the CEO memo, and the dispute thread together, the planted gaps are:

1. **Structured ↔ prose mismatch in deals.** `bonusesJson` exists; `dealNotesFreetext` is what's trusted. The in-app engine only reads the former.
2. **Deal-type coverage.** ~82% of deal types (vs / % of net / door) fall through to a "not supported" empty state.
3. **Recoups are their own lifecycle.** Disputed recoups can persist after the rest of the settlement is signed; recoup-line ambiguity is the single biggest dispute driver (per the Coastal Spell thread).
4. **Comps that count toward gross** vary per deal — another quiet source of friction.
5. **Settlement state machine** has 9 statuses; the UI surfaces them but does nothing to help the user move between them.
6. **Time gate.** Future shows live in the DB but the query layer hides them — recent commit history (`5105d5e`, `8692708`, `d910368`) shows this was deliberately tightened.

---

## 12. Suggested entry points when reading

If you're new to this repo, read in roughly this order:

1. [README.md](README.md) — quickstart + file map (you've likely already read it).
2. [data/ceo-memo.md](data/ceo-memo.md) — strategic frame.
3. [db/schema.ts](db/schema.ts) — the data model, comments and all.
4. [lib/dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts) — top comment + body. This is the seam.
5. [app/shows/[id]/settle/page.tsx](app/shows/%5Bid%5D/settle/page.tsx) — how the seam surfaces to the user.
6. [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md) — what the seam costs.
7. [data/transcripts/](data/transcripts/) — what users say about it.
8. [lib/queries.ts](lib/queries.ts) and [app/reports/page.tsx](app/reports/page.tsx) — how the org sees it in aggregate.

Wrote [REPO_OVERVIEW.md](REPO_OVERVIEW.md) at the repo root. Highlights of what it covers:

- **What it is** — case-study starter codebase for Greenroom, a venue-ops product, seeded as The Crescent (Nashville, 650-cap), with the candidate logged in as booker Mariana Reyes.
- **Stack** — Next.js 16 App Router + React 19 + TS5, Tailwind 4 (theme-in-CSS), Drizzle ORM over libsql (file-based SQLite committed at `data/greenroom.db`), Fraunces + Geist fonts.
- **Routes** — `/shows`, `/shows/[id]`, `/shows/[id]/settle`, `/artists`, `/reports`, `/context`, with a global ⌘K command palette and a fixed sidebar.
- **Database** — 11 tables documented from [db/schema.ts](db/schema.ts) (users, venues, agencies, agents, artists, shows, deals, ticketSales, comps, expenses, settlements), with the structured-vs-prose deal seam and the 9-state settlement machine called out.
- **lib/** — [queries.ts](lib/queries.ts) (past-shows-only time gate), [dealMath.ts](lib/dealMath.ts) (deliberately incomplete engine — handles only `flat` + `percentage_of_gross`), [settlementStage.ts](lib/settlementStage.ts), [format.ts](lib/format.ts).
- **Design tokens** — canvas/brand/ink/amber/rose/sky scales from [globals.css](app/globals.css), plus the paper-grain `::after` overlay and `.eyebrow`/`.tabular` utilities.
- **Narrative substrate** — [data/ceo-memo.md](data/ceo-memo.md), [data/dispute-thread.md](data/dispute-thread.md) (the hand-seeded Coastal Spell show), four user-research transcripts.
- **The planted seams** — 6 enumerated case-study gaps (structured↔prose mismatch, ~82% unsupported deal types, recoup lifecycle, comps-toward-gross, state-machine UX, future-show time gate).
- **Suggested reading order** for someone new to the repo.

content.text:
Created [db-explorer.html](db-explorer.html) at the repo root. It's a single static file — no Next.js, no build step, no server dependency on the app.

**How to use it**

Because browsers block `fetch()` over `file://`, auto-loading `data/greenroom.db` only works if you serve the directory. Two options:

```bash
# from the repo root
python3 -m http.server 8000
# then open http://localhost:8000/db-explorer.html
```

…or just double-click the HTML and use the **Open .db** button (or drag-and-drop the `.db` file onto the page). All three paths work.

**What's in it**

- **sql.js (WASM SQLite) via CDN** — runs the actual SQLite engine in your browser; nothing leaves your machine.
- **Sidebar** — all 11 tables with live row counts (`shows 537`, `expenses 2943`, `comps 1935`, …), plus a Schema overview and a SQL editor.
- **Table view** — paged (50/100/250/500/1000), sortable by clicking any column header, free-text filter across all columns, page CSV export.
- **Schema view** — every table as a card with column name, type, PK / NOT NULL flags; click any row to jump to that table's data.
- **SQL editor** — full Cmd/Ctrl+Enter execution, side panel of 11 pre-written queries tuned to this DB's seams: disputed settlements, deal-type mix, deals carrying structured bonuses, lifecycle counts, top-grossing shows, comps that count toward gross, recoup JSON, artist show counts, **agent dispute rate**, expense $ by category, and the hand-seeded Coastal Spell dispute.
- **Smart cell rendering optimized for this DB**:
  - `bonuses_json` / `recoups_json` / `calculation_json` — pretty-printed JSON in a code-styled cell.
  - `status` / `deal_type` / `category` / `role` — color-coded pills (disputed/voided → rose, paid/signed → brand, revised → amber, submitted → sky).
  - Timestamp columns (Drizzle ms epoch) → human-readable `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM Z`.
  - Money-looking numeric columns → USD format.
  - Booleans (`counts_toward_gross`, `approved`, `absorbed_by_venue`) → TRUE/false visual weight.
  - NULLs italicized so the gaps are visible.
- **Row drawer** — click any row for a full-field inspector (slides in from the right, JSON expanded).
- **Keyboard** — Cmd/Ctrl+K jumps to the SQL editor; Cmd/Ctrl+Enter runs.
- **Open arbitrary .db** — the "Open .db" button or drag-and-drop lets you point it at any SQLite file, not just this project's.

The page is deliberately self-contained: one HTML file, one CDN script tag, no other dependencies, no relation to the Next.js app.
what is the counts towards grosss column used for, point me at it

`counts_toward_gross` is a per-row flag on the **`comps`** table. It controls whether a category of comp tickets should be added to gross box office when the artist's percentage is calculated.

**Where it's defined**

- [db/schema.ts:174-176](db/schema.ts#L174-L176) — the column on the `comps` table, default `false`. The schema comment at [db/schema.ts:147-152](db/schema.ts#L147-L152) frames it directly:

  > A real source of dispute — agents sometimes argue that certain comps "should have counted toward the gross" the artist's % is calculated against. Most comps don't count, but the rules vary by category and sometimes by deal.

**Where it's read in the UI**

- [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:72-74](app/shows/%5Bid%5D/page.tsx#L72-L74) — totals up `compsCountingTowardGross` for the show detail page.
- [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:346-353](app/shows/%5Bid%5D/page.tsx#L346-L353) — the show detail's Comps card surfaces "*N* count toward gross" in amber when any do.
- [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:388-395](app/shows/%5Bid%5D/page.tsx#L388-L395) — the per-row "Counts toward gross?" Yes/No column inside that same table.

**Where it's seeded**

- [db/seed.ts](db/seed.ts) — search for `countsTowardGross` to see the per-category logic that decides which categories get marked true at seed time.

**Where it's *not* used (and this is the seam)**

- [lib/dealMath.ts:23](lib/dealMath.ts#L23) — the settlement engine's docstring explicitly lists `comps that count toward gross` under "It does NOT handle". So the flag is displayed and queryable, but the in-app math ignores it — exactly the kind of gap the case study is pointing at.

Tip: in [db-explorer.html](db-explorer.html), the **"Comps that count toward gross"** quick-query in the SQL editor pulls every row where this flag is true, joined to show + artist.
